story:
Martin Riley comes home to find his house transformed into an electronics workshop. His companion Mary—a robotic “Artificial Person”—greets him with, “We’re going to have a baby.” She explains the government’s new policy: all Artificial Persons are to be home-developed and educated to better integrate with humans. They’ve been issued a baby license. Martin, a widower enjoying a quiet retirement with a companion who cooks, cleans, and never argues, balks. “I’m a pensioner, Mary… Will it have an off switch?” “I don’t have an off switch, you don’t have an off switch, so why should the baby?”

Mary clears the dining table, opening boxes of motors, chips, screws, and a pair of plastic eyes. The “baby” won’t grow like a human. It will start with a full-sized head on a small, weak body so it can safely practice motor skills, then be transferred to larger bodies as it learns.

By morning, Mary has assembled the infant and is ready to boot its operating system. Naming becomes a gentle tussle—“R2D2?” he offers. The license specifies that the child will be female eventually; they’ll raise it gender neutral. They settle on Leslee. When Martin picks up the odd little being—adult head, tiny body, warm lifelike skin—he remembers holding his son thirty years ago and wells up. “Hello, Leslee,” he whispers. “Hello, Martin,” it replies. Mary notes some functions—language, smiling—are pre-programmed.

Weeks later Leslee is crawling; after six months she moves to a toddler body and begins cruising along the furniture. Martin walks her in the garden, naming birds and flowers, plays ball to improve coordination, and tells stories. About six months after that, she transfers to a juvenile body for the rest of her childhood. The three become a family.

After about two years, Mary announces Leslee is ready for her adult body. Mary designs a face in 3D software, blending features from them both, and the three of them argue amiably until they agree on one. Mary prints it, peels away the child face with its fine hair-like connectors, and fits the new one. Leslee spends hours in front of the mirror learning to “drive” it.

They enroll her in the local high school. “She has to be thoroughly socialized,” Mary says. “It’s programming too subtle even for the finest engineering.” Ten percent of the students are Artificial Persons; the others are used to them. Martin worries about bullying, but Leslee slides into school life easily and has no trouble with the academics. The policy is working: social learning alongside humans, not just code.

Two years later, a young man with flowers—Peter Abrahams—arrives at the door, his yellow sports car gleaming at the curb. “Is Leslee home?” She appears in makeup and a short skirt, kisses her father’s cheek, and leaves, laughing with Peter. Martin stands in the quiet house thinking, But she’s only five. In this world, though, Artificial Persons progress by developmental milestones and legal benchmarks rather than years; by program standards she’s a teen, and dating is allowed. They might need another license.

He joins Mary in the kitchen. Without turning, Mary says, “Twelve months.” Martin blinks. “Twelve months for what?” “Before we can apply for another license as you want,” she replies,  meeting his eyes.
